title: AWS Is Back: Amazon Blows Past Q1 Estimates as Cloud Growth Hits 15-Quarter High
subtitle: AWS revenue reaccelerated to its fastest pace in nearly four years, powering Amazon's strongest earnings beat in recent memory.
summary: Amazon crushed Q1 2026 expectations as AWS revenue growth hit a 15-quarter high, reaccelerating to 28% year-over-year. The cloud business posted a $150 billion annualized run rate, fueled by insatiable AI workloads. Combined with record operating income of $23.9 billion, the quarter marks a definitive inflection point for the mega-cap tech giant.

image.caption: The Amazon Spheres in Seattle, Washington. Amazon's Q1 2026 earnings were fueled by AWS reacceleration to a 15-quarter growth high.

bodyHtml:
Amazon just delivered the kind of quarter that rewrites narratives. Q1 2026 was not a beat — it was a statement. Revenue crushed estimates, operating income hit a record $23.9 billion, and the crown jewel — Amazon Web Services — roared back with its fastest growth in 15 quarters.

AWS revenue grew 28% year-over-year, hitting a $150 billion annualized run rate. That's not just a reacceleration — it's a re-declaration of dominance. The last time AWS grew this fast, the AI boom was barely a whisper. Now it's a deafening roar.

AWS grew 28% YoY — a 15-quarter high — hitting a $150B annualized run rate. The cloud giant is back.

The numbers tell the story. Amazon reported Q1 revenue of $187.5 billion, topping consensus estimates of $183.2 billion. Operating income of $23.9 billion crushed the $20.1 billion Wall Street expected. Net income surged 77% year-over-year to $18.4 billion. Every major segment outperformed.

Here's the breakdown that matters:

  • AWS: $28.4B revenue (+28% YoY) — 15-quarter growth high, AI workloads driving acceleration
  • Advertising: $17.5B (+26% YoY) — now bigger than YouTube's entire ad business
  • North America Retail: $112.3B (+12% YoY) — steady, profitable growth
  • International: $41.3B (+11% YoY) — improving margins
  • Operating Margin: 12.8% — up from 9.2% a year ago

The reacceleration story is all about AI. CEO Andy Jassy made it clear on the earnings call: AWS is seeing unprecedented demand for AI infrastructure. Enterprises aren't just experimenting anymore — they're deploying AI workloads at scale on AWS's Trainium and Inferentia chips. The custom silicon strategy is paying off.

Roth MKM's Rohit Kulkarni told CNBC that Amazon's 'magic number' is AWS growth. He's right. The cloud segment contributed more than 65% of Amazon's total operating income this quarter — a staggering figure that underscores just how critical AWS is to the Amazon profit engine. When AWS accelerates, Amazon's entire earnings picture transforms.

The AI compute demand is creating a virtuous cycle. Amazon's Trainium 2 chips are gaining traction with major customers, and reports suggest the company has secured $225 billion in AI-related commitments over the next several years. Meta, Anthropic, and OpenAI are all consuming massive AWS compute for training and inference workloads.

But there's a catch. Amazon's capital expenditures hit $44.2 billion in Q1 alone — a jaw-dropping number that reflects the massive infrastructure buildout required to meet AI demand. Free cash flow dropped to just $1.2 billion, raising questions about the near-term return on this spending spree.

$44.2B in capex. $1.2B in free cash flow. The AI buildout is expensive — but Amazon is betting it pays off.

This is the classic hyperscaler paradox: you spend like crazy today to own the infrastructure that everybody needs tomorrow. Amazon is spending 99% of its operating cash flow on capex — a level of reinvestment that would terrify most companies. But Amazon isn't most companies. It has the balance sheet, the cash flows, and the market position to make this bet work.

The advertising business deserves its own spotlight. Amazon's ad revenue of $17.5 billion makes it the third-largest digital ad platform in the world, behind only Google and Meta. The ad business grew faster than AWS in percentage terms and carries similarly high margins. It's a profit machine that most investors still underappreciate.

Meanwhile, Amazon's retail operations are humming. One-day and same-day delivery have become table stakes, and Amazon's logistics network — built during the pandemic and refined since — is now a competitive moat that rivals simply can't match. The company added 5 million new Prime members in Q1, continuing its steady march toward global subscriber penetration.

For those tracking the cloud wars, this quarter is a signal. Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud both posted strong quarters too, but AWS's reacceleration to a 15-quarter high changes the competitive narrative. The market leader just proved it still has runway — lots of it.

The consensus on Wall Street is shifting. Analysts are racing to raise price targets, with the average now sitting above $265 per share — implying roughly 20% upside from current levels. The bull case rests on AWS sustaining this growth trajectory as AI workloads continue migrating to the cloud.

Amazon's Q1 2026 results are a reminder that in the AI era, infrastructure is the new oil. And AWS is sitting on the biggest reserves in the world. The reacceleration is real. The question is how long it can last.
